What is Campus Market?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "Campus Market is an online student marketplace built for university students in Cameroon. It lets students buy, sell, and rent items — laptops, textbooks, phones, furniture, clothing, and more.

Key features:
• Post listings to sell or rent your items — it's free
• Browse thousands of listings from students across Cameroon
• Filter by category, price, location, and university
• Message sellers directly
• Sasha AI assistant (me!) helps you find the right products
• Seller ratings to build trust

The goal is to make student life more affordable by connecting students who have things to sell with those who need them.", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I create a listing?", "How do I find items near my university?", "Is Campus Market free?"]}

How do I create a listing on Campus Market?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "Creating a listing is free and takes under 5 minutes:

1. Sign in to your Campus Market account
2. Click 'Post a Listing' or the '+' button
3. Fill in: Title, Category, Type (Sell/Rent), Price in XAF, Condition, Description, Location, University
4. Upload photos — at least 3 clear shots from different angles
5. Click 'Publish' — your listing goes live immediately

Tips for a great listing:
• Be honest about condition
• Mention what's included (accessories, box, charger)
• Set a fair price — check similar listings first
• Respond to messages quickly
• Good photos get 3× more views than bad ones", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I take good photos?", "How do I price my item?", "How do I edit a listing?"]}

How do I buy an item on Campus Market?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "Buying on Campus Market is straightforward:

1. Browse or search for what you need
2. Filter by price, location, and university
3. Read the listing carefully — check all photos
4. Click 'Message Seller' to ask questions or express interest
5. Negotiate if needed
6. Agree on a safe public meeting place (library, campus gate, busy café)
7. Inspect the item thoroughly before paying
8. Pay and collect

Safety rules:
• Never pay before seeing the item
• Meet in public during daylight
• Bring a friend for expensive items", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I negotiate a price?", "How do I stay safe meeting a seller?", "How do I report a bad seller?"]}

How do I message a seller?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "To message a seller:

1. Open the listing you're interested in
2. Click 'Message Seller' or 'Contact'
3. Type your message and send
4. View all conversations in the Messages section of your profile

What to say:
• Confirm availability: 'Hi! Is this still available?'
• Ask about condition: 'Does the battery hold charge well?'
• Make an offer: 'Would you accept 45,000 XAF?'
• Arrange meeting: 'I'm at UB. Can we meet at the library gate?'

Tip: Be polite and respond promptly — sellers are students too.", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I negotiate?", "Where are my messages?", "How do I report a suspicious seller?"]}

How do I stay safe on Campus Market?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "Safety tips for buyers and sellers:

FOR BUYERS:
• Meet in a public place — library, campus gate, busy café
• Never pay upfront before seeing the item
• Test electronics before paying
• Bring a friend for high-value purchases

FOR SELLERS:
• Meet in public, well-lit places during daylight
• Collect payment before handing over the item
• Don't share your home address
• Verify mobile money receipt — check your balance, not just a screenshot

RED FLAGS:
• Seller refuses to meet and asks for advance payment
• Price is suspiciously far below market value
• Seller sends a payment link in chat
• Brand new account with no reviews", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I report a scam?", "How does the rating system work?", "What payment methods are safe?"]}

How do I report a scam or suspicious listing?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "To report a scam or suspicious listing:

Report a listing:
1. Open the suspicious listing
2. Click the three-dot menu (⋮) or 'Report' button
3. Select the reason (Scam, Misleading, Counterfeit, Inappropriate)
4. Add details and submit

Report a user:
1. Go to their profile
2. Click 'Report User'
3. Describe the issue and submit

Common scams to watch for:
• Asking for advance payment without meeting
• Fake or counterfeit products
• Bait-and-switch (shows good item, delivers broken one)

The Campus Market team reviews all reports. Document everything with screenshots.", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I stay safe?", "How does the rating system work?", "Can I get a refund?"]}

Can I rent items instead of buying?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "Yes! Campus Market supports both Buy and Rent listings.

Renting is great for:
• Items you need temporarily (event furniture, projector for a presentation)
• Expensive items not worth buying (camera for one shoot)
• Testing before committing to buy

To find rentals:
1. Browse any category
2. Filter by Type: Rent

Before renting, agree on:
• Duration and total price
• Deposit amount
• Condition at return
• What happens if item gets damaged

Common rental items: cameras, projectors, formal wear for ceremonies, event chairs/tables, books for one semester.", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I post a rent listing?", "What deposit is normal for electronics?", "Can I rent a camera?"]}

Is Campus Market free to use?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "Yes — Campus Market is completely free for students!

What's free:
• Creating an account
• Posting listings (buy, sell, or rent)
• Browsing and searching all listings
• Messaging sellers and buyers
• Using Sasha AI assistant
• Accessing chat history and saved items

AI usage: Sasha has a daily usage limit for free users — enough for normal use. It resets the next day.

Payments: Campus Market doesn't charge a commission. All money goes directly between buyer and seller (cash or mobile money).", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I create an account?", "How do I post my first listing?", "What is the AI usage limit?"]}

What payment methods are accepted?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "Campus Market facilitates connections — payments happen directly between buyer and seller.

Cash (most common):
• Exchange at the meeting point
• Count together before handing over the item
• Safest — no delays or reversals

Mobile Money:
• MTN MoMo or Orange Money
• Wait for confirmation SMS before releasing the item
• Verify in your own MoMo account — don't trust screenshots alone
• Confirm sender name matches the buyer

Never use:
• Advance transfers to someone you haven't met
• Unknown bank accounts or foreign services
• QR codes or links sent in chat (potential phishing)", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How does MTN MoMo work?", "How do I stay safe?", "How do I negotiate price?"]}

How do I negotiate price on Campus Market?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "Negotiating is completely normal on Campus Market!

For buyers:
• Start 10–20% below the listed price
• Be polite and brief: 'I'm a student on a tight budget, would you accept 40,000 XAF? I can meet today.'
• Offer something: quick meeting, cash payment, picking up the item yourself

For sellers:
• List slightly above your minimum to leave negotiation room
• Don't drop too fast — it signals you'll go lower
• Add value instead of just cutting price: 'I'll include the charger and mouse'

Sample negotiation:
Buyer: 'Would you take 45,000 for the laptop?'
Seller: 'Best I can do is 50,000 — it comes with the original charger and bag.'
Buyer: 'Deal! Can we meet tomorrow at 2pm at the library gate?'", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I message a seller?", "Is the price fair?", "What's a good price for a second-hand laptop?"]}

How does the seller rating system work?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "The rating system builds trust between buyers and sellers.

How it works:
• After a transaction, both buyer and seller can leave a 1–5 star rating + written review
• Your overall rating is the average of all received ratings
• Ratings are visible on every user's profile
• Top-rated sellers appear higher in search results

How to earn good ratings:
• Be honest about the item's condition
• Respond to messages quickly and politely
• Show up on time for meetings
• Make the transaction smooth and friendly

Aim for: 4.5 stars or above, 80%+ response rate, complete profile.", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I get more views?", "How do I report a bad review?", "How do I become a top seller?"]}

How do I edit or delete my listing?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "Edit a listing:
1. Profile → My Listings
2. Click the Edit (pencil icon) button
3. Update title, price, description, photos, etc.
4. Click Save Changes

Delete a listing:
1. Profile → My Listings
2. Click Delete (trash icon) or three-dot menu
3. Confirm deletion

Tip: Instead of deleting, mark items as 'Sold'. This keeps your transaction history and helps your seller rating. Buyers see you're an active, reliable seller.", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How does the rating system work?", "How do I get more views?", "How do I re-list a sold item?"]}

How do I get more views and sell faster?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "Tips to sell faster on Campus Market:

Great photos (biggest impact):
• Shoot in natural daylight near a window
• Show all angles and any damage honestly
• For electronics: show the screen turned on
• Use 4–6 photos minimum

Clear searchable title:
• Include brand, model, specs, location: 'Samsung A32 – 128GB – Buea'
• Avoid vague titles like 'Nice phone for sale'

Competitive pricing:
• Search similar items and price at or slightly below
• Add 'Price negotiable' in the description

Share your listing:
• Post to WhatsApp student groups at your university
• Share to your class or department group chat

Respond quickly:
• First to respond often gets the sale", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I take good listing photos?", "How do I price my item?", "How do I create a listing?"]}

How do I register on Campus Market?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "Creating an account is free and takes about 2 minutes:

1. Go to the Campus Market website
2. Click 'Register' or 'Sign Up'
3. Enter: name, email, password, university, city
4. Verify your email — click the confirmation link sent to your inbox
5. Complete your profile: add a photo, phone number, bio

Tips for a trustworthy profile:
• Use your real name and a clear photo — real profiles get more responses
• Add your university — makes your listings locally relevant
• Add a phone number — sellers who can be reached by WhatsApp get more buyers", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I post my first listing?", "How do I find items to buy?", "Is Campus Market free?"]}

How do I reset my password?

{"intent": "platform_help", "assistant_message": "To reset your Campus Market password:

1. Go to the Login page
2. Click 'Forgot Password?'
3. Enter the email address on your account
4. Check your inbox for a password reset email (check spam too!)
5. Click the reset link and enter a new password
6. Log in with the new password

Notes:
• Reset links expire after 30–60 minutes — use promptly
• If no email arrives in 5 minutes, check your spam/junk folder
• Contact Campus Market support if still stuck", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I update my profile?", "How do I change my phone number?", "How do I delete my account?"]}

How do I decorate my student room on a budget?

{"intent": "room_setup", "assistant_message": "Decorating a student room on a budget is totally doable! Here's a practical approach:

WALLS:
• Hang a few affordable posters or printed photos (5,000–15,000 XAF total)
• A simple mirror makes any room look bigger and brighter
• Use removable adhesive strips — no damage to walls

LIGHTING:
• Replace the harsh overhead bulb with a warm LED bulb (3,000 XAF)
• Add a small desk lamp for atmosphere and study comfort
• String/fairy lights around the headboard create a cozy feel for 3,000–8,000 XAF

TEXTILES:
• A colorful throw blanket on the bed changes the whole feel (8,000–15,000 XAF)
• A small rug on the floor adds warmth (10,000–20,000 XAF)
• Matching pillow covers tie the look together (3,000–6,000 XAF)

ORGANIZATION:
• Clear storage boxes under the bed
• A small shelf for books and personal items
• Cable organizers keep the desk tidy

Color tip: Pick 2–3 colors and stick to them. Neutral base (white, beige, grey) + 1–2 accent colors.", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What furniture do I need for a student room?", "Best colors for a small room?", "Help me find decor on Campus Market"]}

What furniture do I need for a student room?

{"intent": "room_setup", "assistant_message": "Here's the essential furniture for a complete student room, in priority order:

MUST-HAVES:
1. Bed/Foam/Mattress — your most important piece. Foam on a wooden base works well.
2. Study desk — at least 60cm deep, enough for laptop + books
3. Chair — get one with back support for long study sessions
4. Storage — wardrobe or hanging rail + drawers/boxes for clothes
5. Bookshelf or wall-mounted shelves

NICE-TO-HAVE:
• Small bedside table
• Mirror (wall or standing)
• Small rug
• Curtains/blinds (important for sleep quality)
• Fan (essential in warm cities like Douala and Yaoundé)

Budget estimate for second-hand furniture:
Foam/mattress: 30,000–60,000 XAF
Desk: 20,000–40,000 XAF
Chair: 10,000–25,000 XAF
Storage: 15,000–35,000 XAF", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["Help me furnish a room for 200,000 XAF", "How do I decorate a small room?", "Where to find furniture on Campus Market?"]}

What are the best colors for a student bedroom?

{"intent": "room_setup", "assistant_message": "Color can completely change how a room feels. Here's a guide:

FOR A CALM, FOCUSED STUDY SPACE:
• Soft blue or teal — promotes calm and focus
• Sage green — relaxing and easy on the eyes
• Light grey + white — clean, minimal, professional

FOR A WARM, COZY VIBE:
• Warm beige or terracotta — earthy and welcoming
• Mustard yellow accents — energetic without being harsh
• Warm white walls + wood tones

FOR A BOLD, ENERGETIC ROOM:
• Deep green feature wall + neutral furniture
• Navy blue + white + gold accents
• Black + white with one color pop (orange, yellow, red)

PRACTICAL TIPS:
• If you can't paint (rented room), use colored textiles (blankets, cushions, rugs) to add color
• Stick to 2–3 colors maximum
• Lighter colors make small rooms feel bigger
• Dark colors make large rooms feel cozier", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I decorate a small room?", "What textiles should I buy?", "Help me find bedding on Campus Market"]}

How do I decorate a parlor or living room on a budget?

{"intent": "room_setup", "assistant_message": "A great parlor/living room doesn't require a big budget. Here's how:

SEATING (highest impact):
• A second-hand 3-seater sofa: 40,000–80,000 XAF
• Or 2 armchairs instead — more flexible layout
• Throw pillows in matching colors (3,000–6,000 XAF each)

CENTER PIECE:
• Small coffee table or center table (15,000–35,000 XAF)
• A decorative tray on top with a plant, candles, or books

WALLS:
• A large framed poster or artwork as the focal point
• Gallery wall of 3–5 smaller frames arranged in a grid
• A mirror to reflect light and make the room feel larger

LIGHTING:
• Replace harsh bulbs with warm white LEDs
• A floor lamp in a corner creates ambiance

RUG:
• A rug anchors the seating area and ties everything together
• It's one of the highest-impact purchases for a living room

PLANTS:
• A small plant or two adds life and freshness (3,000–8,000 XAF)", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What furniture does a parlor need?", "How do I choose a rug?", "Find me sofa options on Campus Market"]}

How do I make a small room look bigger?

{"intent": "room_setup", "assistant_message": "These tricks make any small room look and feel more spacious:

LIGHT:
• Use light, bright colors on walls — white, cream, pale blue
• Maximize natural light — keep windows unobstructed
• Use multiple light sources instead of one overhead light

MIRRORS:
• A large mirror on one wall doubles the visual space
• Place mirror opposite a window to reflect natural light

FURNITURE:
• Fewer, multi-purpose pieces (storage ottoman, desk that folds)
• Furniture with legs looks airier than pieces that sit on the floor
• Avoid oversized furniture — scale matters
• Use vertical space: tall shelves draw the eye up

ORGANIZATION:
• Clutter makes small rooms feel tiny — keep surfaces clear
• Under-bed storage boxes maximize hidden space
• One color scheme throughout feels more cohesive and less cramped

CURTAINS:
• Hang curtains high (near the ceiling) and wide — makes windows look larger", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What furniture fits a small room?", "Best colors for small rooms?", "How do I organize a small room?"]}

What should I put on my room walls?

{"intent": "room_setup", "assistant_message": "Wall decoration ideas for a student room:

AFFORDABLE OPTIONS:
• Printed photos — print your favorite memories at a photo shop (500–1,000 XAF each)
• Posters — motivation quotes, maps, art, favorite music/sports
• A mirror — functional AND decorative, adds light
• Wall clock — practical and decorative

GALLERY WALL:
• Pick 4–8 frames in matching or complementary sizes
• Arrange on the floor first before hanging
• Mix photos, art prints, and small mirrors
• Keep 5–8cm between frames for a clean look

ZERO-COST IDEAS:
• Pin fabric or a bright scarf as wall art
• Tape string across the wall and clip photos with pegs
• Use old textbooks stacked on a shelf as display

IMPORTANT: If you're renting, use removable adhesive strips (like 3M Command strips) to avoid damaging walls.   - Bottom: 2–3 sentence summary — fill in within 24 hours

AFTER THE LECTURE:
• Review and rewrite your notes within 24 hours — memory fades fast
• Add diagrams, examples, and connections to other topics
• Turn your notes into questions you could answer on an exam

What is photosynthesis?

{"intent": "science_qa", "assistant_message": "Photosynthesis is the process by which plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy stored as glucose.

THE EQUATION:
6CO₂ + 6H₂O + light energy → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂

Plants absorb CO₂ from air and water from soil. Chlorophyll in chloroplasts absorbs sunlight to power the reaction, producing glucose (food) and oxygen as a by-product.

TWO STAGES:
• Light-dependent reactions — occur in thylakoid membranes, use light to split water and produce ATP
• Light-independent reactions (Calvin cycle) — use ATP to convert CO₂ into glucose

WHY IT MATTERS:
• Produces the oxygen we breathe
• Forms the base of almost every food chain on Earth
• Drives the carbon cycle", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What is cellular respiration?", "Difference between photosynthesis and respiration?", "What is chlorophyll?"]}

What is Newton's second law of motion?

{"intent": "science_qa", "assistant_message": "Newton's Second Law: F = ma

Force = mass × acceleration

• F = net force (Newtons, N)
• m = mass (kilograms, kg)
• a = acceleration (m/s²)

In plain terms: the greater the force on an object, the greater its acceleration. The heavier the object, the more force needed for the same acceleration.

EXAMPLE:
Push a 5 kg box with 20 N of force:
a = F ÷ m = 20 ÷ 5 = 4 m/s²

REAL-WORLD APPLICATIONS:
• Car acceleration depends on engine force and vehicle mass
• Rocket thrust must exceed the rocket's weight × desired acceleration
• Why heavy trucks brake more slowly than cars", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What is Newton's first law?", "What is momentum?", "Explain the concept of force in physics"]}

What is the Pythagorean theorem?

{"intent": "math_help", "assistant_message": "The Pythagorean Theorem: a² + b² = c²

In a right-angled triangle:
• a and b = the two shorter sides (legs)
• c = hypotenuse (longest side, opposite the right angle)

EXAMPLE:
Legs = 3 cm and 4 cm. Find the hypotenuse:
c² = 3² + 4² = 9 + 16 = 25
c = √25 = 5 cm

Common Pythagorean triples: 3-4-5, 5-12-13, 8-15-17

REAL-WORLD USES:
• Construction (checking right angles)
• Navigation (shortest distance)

What is compound interest?

{"intent": "general_qa", "assistant_message": "Compound interest is interest earned on both the principal AND the accumulated interest from previous periods. Your interest earns interest — it snowballs.

FORMULA: A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt)
• A = Final amount
• P = Principal (initial amount)
• r = Annual interest rate (decimal)
• n = Times interest compounds per year
• t = Years

EXAMPLE:
Save 100,000 XAF at 10% annual, compounded yearly for 5 years:
A = 100,000 × (1.10)^5 = 161,051 XAF
Simple interest would give only 150,000 XAF — compound earned 11,051 XAF extra!

KEY LESSON:
Start saving early — even small amounts grow significantly over time. Starting 10 years earlier can double your final amount.", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I start saving as a student?", "What is inflation?", "How do I manage my budget?"]}

{"intent": "student_advice", "assistant_message": "GPA = Σ(Grade Points × Credit Hours) ÷ Total Credit Hours

{"intent": "general_qa", "assistant_message": "Malaria is caused by Plasmodium parasites — most commonly P. falciparum in sub-Saharan Africa including Cameroon.

HOW IT SPREADS:
Through the bite of infected female Anopheles mosquitoes. The mosquito injects parasites into your bloodstream. They travel to the liver, multiply, then attack red blood cells.

SYMPTOMS (7–30 days after infection):
• High fever (cycling — often every 48–72 hours)
• Chills and sweating
• Headache
• Muscle aches and fatigue
• Nausea and vomiting

SEVERE MALARIA (EMERGENCY):
• Confusion or unconsciousness
• Difficulty breathing
• Dark urine, jaundice

PREVENTION:
• Sleep under insecticide-treated nets
• Use mosquito repellent
• Eliminate standing water near home

• Keep away from heat and sunlight — a cool, dark shelf is ideal
• Dry staples last months when stored correctly

How do I cook jollof rice?

{"intent": "general_qa", "assistant_message": "Cameroonian jollof rice — here's the recipe (serves 3–4):

INGREDIENTS:
• 2 cups long-grain rice
• 400g tomato paste or 4 fresh tomatoes (blended)
• 2 red bell peppers (blended)
• 1 large onion (diced)
• 3 cloves garlic (crushed)
• 3 cups chicken/vegetable stock or water
• 3 tbsp vegetable oil
• 2 Maggi cubes, salt, pepper to taste
• Optional: bay leaves, thyme, scotch bonnet

STEPS:
1. Heat oil in a heavy pot. Fry onion until golden (5 min)
2. Add blended tomatoes and peppers. Stir-fry 10–15 min on medium heat until oil floats to the top — this step is key!
3. Add garlic, stock, seasoning cubes, salt, and spices
4. Wash rice and add to the pot. Stir everything together
5. Cover and cook on low heat 25–30 min, checking and stirring every 10 min
6. Taste, adjust seasoning. Steam with lid on for 5 more minutes

PRO TIPS:
• Use a heavy-bottomed pot to prevent burning
• Don't lift the lid too often
• The smoky bottom (party jollof effect) is actually desired!", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I cook ndolé?", "What are easy student meals?", "What pots do I need for cooking?"]}

What is the difference between speed and velocity?

{"intent": "science_qa", "assistant_message": "Speed and velocity are related but different concepts:

SPEED:
• Scalar quantity — only has magnitude (size), no direction
• Formula: Speed = Distance ÷ Time
• Example: 'A car is moving at 80 km/h'

VELOCITY:
• Vector quantity — has both magnitude AND direction
• Formula: Velocity = Displacement ÷ Time
• Example: 'A car is moving at 80 km/h due North'

KEY DIFFERENCE:
You can have speed without velocity, but not the other way around.

CLASSIC EXAMPLE:
If you run one lap around a 400m track in 60 seconds:
• Your speed = 400 ÷ 60 = 6.67 m/s
• Your velocity = 0 m/s (because your displacement is zero — you ended where you started!)

This is why velocity uses displacement (shortest path from start to end) while speed uses total distance traveled.", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What is acceleration?", "What is Newton's first law?", "Explain distance vs displacement"]}

How do I solve quadratic equations?

{"intent": "math_help", "assistant_message": "A quadratic equation has the form: ax² + bx + c = 0

THREE METHODS:

1. FACTORING (when possible — fastest):
x² + 5x + 6 = 0
(x + 2)(x + 3) = 0
x = -2 or x = -3

2. QUADRATIC FORMULA (always works):
x = (-b ± √(b² - 4ac)) ÷ 2a

Example: 2x² - 4x - 6 = 0 (a=2, b=-4, c=-6)
x = (4 ± √(16 + 48)) ÷ 4
x = (4 ± √64) ÷ 4
x = (4 ± 8) ÷ 4
x = 3 or x = -1

3. COMPLETING THE SQUARE:
x² + 6x + 5 = 0
(x + 3)² - 9 + 5 = 0
(x + 3)² = 4
x + 3 = ±2
x = -1 or x = -5

THE DISCRIMINANT (b² - 4ac) tells you about solutions:
• > 0: two real solutions
• = 0: one real solution (repeated)
• < 0: no real solutions (complex/imaginary)", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What is the quadratic formula?", "How do I solve simultaneous equations?", "What is calculus?"]}

How does WiFi work?

{"intent": "tech_help", "assistant_message": "WiFi is a wireless networking technology that allows devices to connect to the internet without physical cables.

HOW IT WORKS:
1. Your Internet Service Provider (ISP) delivers internet to your location via a cable
2. A router connects to that cable and converts the signal to radio waves
3. The router broadcasts radio waves in the 2.4GHz or 5GHz frequency bands
4. Your phone, laptop, or tablet has a WiFi adapter that receives those radio waves
5. Data travels back and forth as radio signals between your device and the router

2.4GHz vs 5GHz:
• 2.4GHz — longer range, better through walls, but slower and more congested
• 5GHz — faster speeds, but shorter range (fewer walls it penetrates)

WHY WIFI SLOWS DOWN:
• Too many devices on one router
• Distance from the router
• Walls and obstacles absorbing the signal
• Network congestion during peak hours

TIPS FOR BETTER CAMPUS WIFI:
• Sit closer to access points in the library
• Download large files early morning when fewer users are online
• Use a wired connection (ethernet) for fastest speeds where available", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What is 4G vs 5G?", "How do I secure my WiFi connection?", "What is a VPN?"]}

What is the difference between an invoice and a receipt?

{"intent": "general_qa", "assistant_message": "Both are business documents but they serve different purposes:

INVOICE:
• Sent by the SELLER to the BUYER before or at the time of payment
• A request for payment — 'You owe me X amount for these goods/services'
• Includes: invoice number, itemized list, quantities, prices, payment due date
• Used to initiate a transaction or for credit arrangements

RECEIPT:
• Proof that payment has been MADE
• Issued AFTER payment is received
• Confirms: what was purchased, how much was paid, when it was paid
• Used by buyer as proof of purchase

SIMPLE ANALOGY:
Invoice = the bill at a restaurant (before you pay)
Receipt = the slip you get after paying

RELEVANCE FOR STUDENTS:
When selling items on Campus Market, providing a simple written receipt (date, item, amount, both names) protects both you and the buyer in case of disputes.", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I handle disputes on Campus Market?", "What is a business plan?", "How do I start a small business?"]}

What is Ohm's Law?

{"intent": "science_qa", "assistant_message": "Ohm's Law describes the relationship between voltage, current, and resistance in an electrical circuit:

V = I × R

• V = Voltage (Volts, V) — the electrical pressure driving current
• I = Current (Amperes, A) — the flow of electric charge
• R = Resistance (Ohms, Ω) — how much the circuit opposes the flow

REARRANGED:
• I = V ÷ R (current increases with voltage, decreases with resistance)
• R = V ÷ I

EXAMPLE:
A lamp connected to 220V mains with a resistance of 440 Ω:
I = V ÷ R = 220 ÷ 440 = 0.5 A

REAL-WORLD APPLICATION:
• Why thin wires get hot (high resistance → more heat)
• Why you need the right fuse for appliances
• Designing circuits for electronics projects

POWER (bonus formula): P = V × I = I²R = V²/R", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What is electrical power?", "What is a circuit?", "What is the difference between AC and DC?"]}

What is the difference between mitosis and meiosis?

{"intent": "science_qa", "assistant_message": "Both are types of cell division, but they have very different purposes:

MITOSIS:
• Purpose: growth and repair — produces identical body cells
• Produces: 2 daughter cells, each genetically IDENTICAL to the parent cell
• Chromosome number: maintained (diploid → diploid, 2n → 2n)
• Occurs in: all body (somatic) cells
• Number of divisions: 1

MEIOSIS:
• Purpose: sexual reproduction — produces sex cells (gametes)
• Produces: 4 daughter cells, each genetically UNIQUE (due to crossing over)
• Chromosome number: HALVED (diploid → haploid, 2n → n)
• Occurs in: reproductive organs (testes in males, ovaries in females)
• Number of divisions: 2 (Meiosis I and Meiosis II)

WHY THE CHROMOSOME HALVING MATTERS:
When sperm (n) and egg (n) combine during fertilization, the resulting embryo has the full (2n) chromosome count. Without meiosis, chromosome number would double every generation!", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What is DNA replication?", "What is the cell cycle?", "What is fertilization?"]}

How does a CPU work?

{"intent": "tech_help", "assistant_message": "The CPU (Central Processing Unit) is the brain of a computer. Here's how it works:

THE FETCH-DECODE-EXECUTE CYCLE:
1. FETCH: The CPU retrieves an instruction from memory (RAM)
2. DECODE: The control unit decodes what the instruction means
3. EXECUTE: The ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) carries out the instruction
4. WRITEBACK: The result is stored back to memory or a register
This cycle happens billions of times per second!

KEY COMPONENTS:
• ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) — performs math and logic operations
• Control Unit — directs the flow of data and instructions
• Registers — tiny, ultra-fast storage inside the CPU
• Cache — fast memory between CPU and RAM (L1, L2, L3)

CPU SPECS EXPLAINED:
• Clock speed (e.g., 3.5 GHz) — cycles per second (more = faster generally)
• Cores — independent processing units (4-core = 4 tasks simultaneously)
• Threads — virtual cores; 4-core/8-thread handles 8 tasks
• Cache size — bigger cache = fewer slow RAM accesses", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What's the difference between RAM and ROM?", "What CPU should I look for in a student laptop?", "What is an operating system?"]}

{"intent": "tech_help", "assistant_message": "A smart contract is a self-executing program stored on a blockchain that automatically enforces the terms of an agreement when predetermined conditions are met.

ANALOGY:
Think of a vending machine:
• You insert money + select item (input conditions)
• Machine automatically dispenses the item (automatic execution)
• No human cashier needed (no intermediary)

HOW SMART CONTRACTS WORK:
1. Two parties agree on terms and write them as code
2. Code is deployed to a blockchain (usually Ethereum)
3. When conditions are met, the contract executes automatically
4. Results are recorded on the blockchain — transparent and immutable

REAL APPLICATIONS:
• DeFi (Decentralized Finance) — lending, borrowing without banks
• NFTs — proving digital ownership
• Insurance — automatic payouts when conditions met
• Supply chain — automatic payment when goods are delivered

IN AFRICA:
Smart contracts are being explored for land registry, agricultural insurance, and cross-border payments — particularly relevant in Cameroon's growing fintech space.", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What is blockchain?", "What is Ethereum?", "How do I start learning about blockchain?"]}

What is an API?

{"intent": "tech_help", "assistant_message": "API stands for Application Programming Interface. It's a way for different software systems to talk to each other.

SIMPLE ANALOGY:
Think of a restaurant:
• You (the app) place an order with the waiter (API)
• The waiter goes to the kitchen (another system/server)
• Returns with exactly what you asked for
• You never go to the kitchen directly

HOW APIs WORK IN PRACTICE:
1. Your app sends an HTTP request to an API endpoint
   GET https://api.example.com/listings
2. The API processes the request and queries the database
3. Returns data in a structured format (usually JSON)
4. Your app uses the data to update the UI

REAL EXAMPLES:
• Google Maps API — lets websites embed maps and get directions
• MTN MoMo API — lets apps initiate mobile money payments
• Campus Market API — Sasha (me!) uses the API to get listing data
• Weather APIs — apps fetch current weather data

REST vs GraphQL:
• REST API — most common, URL-based endpoints, returns fixed data
• GraphQL — query exactly the data you need, more flexible", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["How do I build a REST API?", "What is JSON?", "What is the difference between frontend and backend?"]}

What is a for loop in Python?

{"intent": "coding_help", "assistant_message": "A for loop in Python repeats a block of code for each item in a sequence.

SYNTAX:
for variable in sequence:
    # code to run

EXAMPLES:
# Loop through a list
fruits = ['mango', 'banana', 'papaya']
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)
# Output: mango / banana / papaya

# Loop a fixed number of times (range)
for i in range(5):
    print(i)
# Output: 0 1 2 3 4

# Loop with index using enumerate
for i, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(i, fruit)
# Output: 0 mango / 1 banana / 2 papaya

# Loop through a string
for char in 'Sasha':
    print(char)

BREAK and CONTINUE:
break — stops the loop early
continue — skips to the next iteration", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What is a while loop?", "What is a list in Python?", "How do I use functions in Python?"]}

Explain what a database index is

{"intent": "coding_help", "assistant_message": "A database index is a data structure that speeds up data retrieval — like a book's index vs reading every page.

WITHOUT INDEX:
SELECT * FROM students WHERE name = 'Alice';
→ Database scans EVERY row until it finds Alice (full table scan) — O(n)

WITH INDEX ON name:
→ Database jumps directly to Alice's records using a B-tree lookup — O(log n)

HOW TO CREATE:
CREATE INDEX idx_student_name ON students(name);

TRADE-OFFS:
PROS:
• Massively faster SELECT queries on indexed columns
CONS:
• Slower INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE (index must be updated too)
• Uses extra disk space

WHEN TO INDEX:
• Columns used frequently in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY clauses
• High-cardinality columns (many unique values) benefit most
• Don't index small tables — full scan is often faster", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What is SQL?", "What is a primary key?", "What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL?"]}

{"intent": "history_qa", "assistant_message": "World War I (1914–1918), also called 'The Great War,' was a global conflict that killed approximately 20 million people.

• M — Militarism: European powers built massive armies and navies
• A — Alliance system: Two armed blocs (Triple Entente vs Triple Alliance)
• I — Imperialism: Competition over colonies increased tensions
• N — Nationalism: Strong national pride and ethnic tensions (especially in the Balkans)

TRIGGER:
June 28, 1914: Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria-Hungary assassinated in Sarajevo by a Serbian nationalist. This set off a chain reaction through the alliance system.

THE TWO SIDES:
• Allied Powers: France, Britain, Russia, Italy (later), USA (1917)
• Central Powers: Germany, Austria-Hungary, Ottoman Empire, Bulgaria

KEY FEATURES:
• Trench warfare — soldiers lived in trenches for years
• New weapons: machine guns, poison gas, tanks, airplanes

END: November 11, 1918 — Germany surrendered. Treaty of Versailles (1919) punished Germany heavily and sowed seeds for World War II.", "recommended_item_ids": [], "recommendation_reasons": {}, "style_plan": null, "kitchen_list": null, "budget_breakdown": null, "next_questions": ["What caused World War II?", "What was the Cold War?", "How did colonialism affect Africa?"]}
